sample_evidence.quote: A set $\cx$ of objects of a category $\ck$ is called weakly initial if each object $K$ of $\ck$ admits a morphism $X\to K$ with $X\in\cx$.
A category $\ck$ is called approximately complete if, for each diagram $D:\cd\to\ck$, the category of cones $K\to D$ over $D$ has a weakly initial set.
Finally, $\Inj(\cc)$ will denote the full subcategory of $\ck$ consisting of all objects $K$ such that the unique morphism $K\to 1$ to the terminal object of $\ck$ belongs to $\cc^\square$.

The pair $(\cl,\crr)$ is called a factorization system if condition (1) is replaced by \item[(1')] $\crr = \cl^\perp$, $\cl = {}^\perp \crr$. {\em A class $\cc$ of morphisms of a category $\ck$ is called cone-coreflective if, for each morphism $f$ in $\ck$, the comma-category $\cc\downarrow f$ has a weakly terminal set.
A full subcategory $\cl$ of $\ck$ is called weakly reflective in $\ck$ if, for each $K$ in $\ck$, the comma-category $K\downarrow\cl$ has a weakly initial object.
